question: what are the catholic holy days?
results:
Do you mean "holy days of obligation" (days Catholics are obliged to attend Mass) or major Catholic holy days/feasts in general? I'll give both briefly.

What a "holy day of obligation" is
- A day on which Catholics are normally required to attend Mass in addition to Sundays. Which days are obligatory can vary by country because local bishops’ conferences (with approval from the Holy See) can transfer or suppress certain days.

Common holy days of obligation (widely observed)
- January 1 — Solemnity of Mary, Mother of God  
- January 6 — Epiphany (in many places it is transferred to the nearest Sunday)  
- Ascension — 40 days after Easter (in many countries moved to the following Sunday)  
- August 15 — Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary  
- November 1 — All Saints’ Day  
- December 8 — Immaculate Conception of the Blessed Virgin Mary  
- December 25 — Christmas  
- Corpus Christi — (Thursday after Trinity Sunday; in many places moved to a Sunday)

Note
- Sundays are the principal weekly holy day for Catholics.  
- Exact holy days of obligation and any transfers to Sundays differ by country/diocese. For the definitive list that applies to you, check your local diocese or bishops’ conference website.

question: what all does google now do?
results:
Do you mean the old Google Now service (the proactive “cards” feature in Android/Google Search) or the current Google Assistant? I’ll summarize what Google Now used to do and note that most of its features are now part of Google Assistant / the Google app.

Main Google Now features (what it did)
- Proactive information cards: showed timely info without asking, based on your search history, location, calendar and Gmail. Examples:
  - Weather for your location
  - Commute and traffic conditions for home→work (ETA and alternate routes)
  - Next calendar events and reminders
  - Flight status, check‑in times and gate info (pulled from Gmail)
  - Hotel and restaurant reservations (from Gmail)
  - Package tracking (from confirmation emails)
  - Sports scores and schedules for teams you follow
  - Stock quotes and market updates
  - Local places and “nearby” suggestions (restaurants, attractions)
  - Movie times, TV schedules, and music/album/news items based on interests
- Contextual cards: cards triggered by your current location or what you were doing (e.g., restaurant menu when you’re at a venue).
- Voice search and voice actions: speak queries and commands (e.g., “navigate to 123 Main St,” “send a text to John,” “set a reminder”).
- Personalized news and updates: suggested articles and trending stories based on interests.
- Translation, currency conversion, unit conversion and quick answers for many queries.

How it worked
- Pulled data from Gmail, Calendar, Search, Location History and device sensors to anticipate needs.
- Delivered notifications and a scrollable “cards” feed in the Google app / home screen.

Current status
- Google Now as a standalone product was phased out and its functionality merged into Google Assistant and the Google app (Discover/Feed). Most proactive, context-aware features continue under Assistant (and in the Google app’s feed), with richer conversational capabilities.

question: what are the sights to see in madrid?
results:
Here are the top sights and experiences in Madrid, with a short note on each:

- Museo del Prado — Spain’s premier art museum (Velázquez, Goya, Bosch). Tip: free late hours some days.  
- Museo Reina Sofía — modern art museum; home of Picasso’s Guernica.  
- Thyssen-Bornemisza — complements Prado and Reina Sofía with a wide-ranging private collection.  
- Palacio Real (Royal Palace) — grand state rooms and changing of the guard; adjacent Almudena Cathedral.  
- Plaza Mayor — historic central square, cafés and arcades; great for photos and people-watching.  
- Puerta del Sol — city center landmark, Km 0 marker, busy plaza and shopping area.  
- Gran Vía — Madrid’s main avenue for shopping, theaters, and architecture.  
- Parque del Retiro — large park with the Crystal Palace, boating lake, sculptures and gardens.  
- Templo de Debod — an ancient Egyptian temple with one of the best sunset views in the city.  
- Mercado de San Miguel — covered market near Plaza Mayor for tapas and local food.  
- El Rastro — famous open-air flea market (Sundays) in La Latina.  
- Lavapiés and Malasaña — vibrant neighborhoods for nightlife, street art, and alternative culture.  
- Santiago Bernabéu Stadium — home of Real Madrid; stadium tour available.  
- Palacio de Cibeles / Mirador (City Hall) — iconic building with a public viewpoint over the city.  
- Royal Botanical Garden (Real Jardín Botánico) — next to the Prado, peaceful plant collections.  
- Jardines de Sabatini — formal gardens beside the Royal Palace with nice views.  
- CaixaForum and its vertical garden — cultural center with exhibitions and a photogenic exterior garden wall.  
- National Archaeological Museum — artifacts spanning Spain’s history.  
- Matadero Madrid — former slaughterhouse turned contemporary arts complex and events venue.  
- Teleférico de Madrid — cable car offering panoramic views between Parque del Oeste and Casa de Campo.

Recommended day trips (if you have time): Toledo, Segovia (Roman aqueduct and Alcázar), El Escorial, and Aranjuez.

question: what to see in christchurch new zealand?
results:
Christchurch has a mix of gardens, heritage sites, museums, coastal spots and easy day trips. Key things to see and do:

Top attractions
- Christchurch Botanic Gardens & Hagley Park — historic gardens, walking paths and open lawns in the city heart.
- Canterbury Museum — natural history and regional exhibits (next to the Botanic Gardens).
- Avon River/Avon Punting — gentle punts (boat rides) along the river through the gardens and city.
- Christchurch Art Gallery Te Puna o Waiwhetu — contemporary and regional art collections.
- Cardboard Cathedral (Transitional Cathedral) & Cathedral Square — earthquake-era architecture and city focal point (original cathedral is being repaired/rebuilt).
- Quake City / Canterbury Earthquake National Memorial — exhibitions and memorials about the 2010–11 earthquakes.
- Christchurch Gondola & Port Hills — cable car to spectacular views over the city, Lyttelton Harbour and Banks Peninsula; lots of walking trails.
- International Antarctic Centre — interactive exhibits and Antarctic-themed experiences (close to the airport).
- Willowbank Wildlife Reserve or Orana Wildlife Park — see native birds (kiwi) and other wildlife; Orana offers close-up encounters.
- New Regent Street & Riverside Market — cafes, boutique shopping and local food.
- New Brighton Beach & Pier — surf beach with a long pier and seaside cafés.

Recommended day trips
- Akaroa (Banks Peninsula) — charming French-influenced village, harbour cruises and Hector’s dolphins.
- Arthur’s Pass / TranzAlpine train — dramatic alpine scenery; the train is one of the world’s great rail journeys.
- Kaikōura — whale watching and marine life encounters (a longer day trip).

Short visit plan ideas
- Half-day central: Botanic Gardens → Canterbury Museum → punting on the Avon → Riverside Market.
- Half-day views + walk: Gondola → Port Hills walks → drive to Scarborough/New Brighton for sunset.
- Day trip: Akaroa (harbour cruise/dolphin watching) or TranzAlpine to Arthur’s Pass.

Practical tips
- The city changed a lot after the 2010–11 earthquakes; some sites were rebuilt or relocated—check opening status in advance.
- Public transport, bikes and rental cars work well for getting around; many attractions are walkable from the central city.
- Weather can be changeable — bring layers.

question: what are some of leonardo da vinci most famous inventions?
results:
Leonardo’s “inventions” are mostly designs and engineering concepts from his notebooks. Some of his most famous ones are:

- Ornithopter (flapping-wing flying machine) — wing-flapping aircraft inspired by birds.  
- Aerial screw (helical screw) — a spiral rotor often cited as a helicopter precursor.  
- Parachute — a pyramidal parachute design intended to allow a person to descend safely.  
- Armored vehicle (“tank”) — a circular, cannon-armed armored vehicle concept for battlefield use.  
- Self-propelled cart — a spring-driven, programmable cart considered an early automobile/robot.  
- Mechanical knight (automaton) — a humanoid robot able to sit, move its arms and jaw.  
- Diving suit / underwater breathing apparatus — leather suit with breathing tubes for submerged work.  
- Giant crossbow and various siege engines — designs for large-scale military machines.  

Most were sketches and theoretical designs in codices such as the Codex Atlanticus and were not built in his lifetime; some modern reconstructions show parts of the ideas could work.

question: what is the islamic belief?
results:
Broadly, Islamic belief centers on submission to one God (Allah) and the teachings brought by the Prophet Muhammad. Core elements:

Main theological beliefs (Iman)
- Tawhid: absolute oneness of God (Allah).
- Angels: spiritual beings who serve God.
- Revealed scriptures: belief that God revealed guidance, with the Qur'an as the final, authoritative revelation.
- Prophets: God’s messengers (from Adam through Muhammad, who is the final prophet).
- Day of Resurrection and Judgment: life after death and accountability for deeds.
- Divine decree (Qadar): belief that God’s knowledge and will encompass all things (understood differently across traditions).

Five Pillars (central religious practices)
- Shahada: declaration of faith (“There is no god but God; Muhammad is His messenger”).
- Salah: ritual prayer performed five times daily.
- Zakat: obligatory almsgiving/charity.
- Sawm: fasting during the month of Ramadan.
- Hajj: pilgrimage to Mecca, required once if physically and financially able.

There are theological and legal variations (e.g., Sunni and Shia emphases such as the Shia doctrine of the Imamate), but the items above summarize the common core of Islamic belief and practice.

question: who was isaac newton and what did he do?
results:
Sir Isaac Newton (born 4 January 1643 — died 31 March 1727) was an English mathematician, physicist, astronomer and natural philosopher who laid the foundations of classical physics.

Key things he did:
- Formulated the three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation, explaining planetary motion and terrestrial mechanics (mainly presented in Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica, 1687).  
- Helped develop calculus (his “method of fluxions”), independently of Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz.  
- Made major contributions to optics: showed white light is composed of colours (prism experiments) and built the first practical reflecting telescope (the Newtonian telescope).  
- Advanced mathematics (generalized binomial theorem, power series, Newton’s method for roots) and mathematical methods used across science.  
- Served in public roles later in life (Warden/Master of the Royal Mint, Member of Parliament) and was President of the Royal Society; he was knighted in 1705.

He is widely regarded as one of the most influential scientists in history.
